name: Neo-Brutalist
description: A deliberately raw, high-contrast style featuring thick black borders, hard offset drop shadows, saturated or clashing colours, exposed structure, and chunky system-like typography. It rejects polish in favour of bold, attention-grabbing honesty about the interface as a constructed object.
emotional_impression: Bold, confident, irreverent, energetic, and intentionally unpolished.
visual_principles: Thick solid borders and hard, non-blurred offset shadows
Saturated, sometimes intentionally clashing colour blocks
Flat fills with no gradients; structure is exposed rather than smoothed
Visible grid seams and unapologetic asymmetry
accessibility_risks: Clashing saturated colour pairings frequently fail contrast and induce visual stress
Aggressive colour can be hostile to users with visual sensitivities or migraine triggers
Decorative borders and shadows can compete with content focus order
performance_risks: Low; effects are CSS-cheap, though heavy custom fonts can add weight
appropriate_contexts: Brands deliberately signalling anti-corporate, independent, or maker identity
Short-lived campaign or launch pages where memorability beats longevity
Audiences that read the rawness as authenticity
inappropriate_contexts: Regulated or trust-sensitive products (banking, healthcare, government)
High-density data tools where the heavy chrome steals attention from content
Accessibility-critical contexts without a carefully audited palette
evidence: Neo-brutalism surged across Gumroad-era indie sites and design Twitter circa 2021-2024, now widely cloned by templates
Its prevalence in template galleries makes it a recognisable AI/template tell, raising cliche risk
